target.title: Doctor: expose gateway runtime findings
target.body:
## Summary

- adds opt-in structured core Doctor checks for `core/doctor/gateway-health` and `core/doctor/gateway-daemon`
- maps gateway reachability and local daemon service state into `HealthFinding` records
- redacts sensitive Gateway URL material before emitting the gateway health finding `target`
- keeps real Gateway service install/start/restart behavior in the existing legacy Doctor run path

## Behavior

- default `doctor --lint` does not run these checks because both are `defaultEnabled: false`
- explicit `--only core/doctor/gateway-health`, `--only core/doctor/gateway-daemon`, or `--all` can surface the findings
- no config, plugin, or SDK public contract changes

## Validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts --reporter=verbose` (99 passed)
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`

## Real behavior proof

Local source CLI, Windows worktree, no local Gateway service running:

- `node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/gateway-daemon`
  - exit code: 1
  - result: `ok:false`, `checksRun:1`, one `core/doctor/gateway-daemon` warning: `Gateway service is not installed.`
- `node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/gateway-health`
  - exit code: 1
  - result: `ok:false`, `checksRun:1`, one `core/doctor/gateway-health` warning: `Gateway is not reachable: connect ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:18789`

Focused regression proof on current head covers a remote Gateway URL containing userinfo and a sensitive query token. The structured finding target is redacted as `wss://***:***@gateway.example.test/rpc?token=***&safe=value`, and the serialized findings do not contain the original userinfo or token value.

Attempted default `node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --json`, but the local source command timed out in this fresh worktree. The new checks are default-disabled and covered by focused lint-flow tests plus explicit `--only` CLI proof above.


## Follow-up Fix
- Head `981ba1906fa4ae27d9e20c3c99646fd9ebbf02b2` also points remote Gateway health findings at `gateway.remote.url` instead of the nonexistent `gateway.url`, covering both prepare-failure and unreachable findings.

target.title: feat: Provider circuit breaker for quota exhaustion
target.body:
Resolves #64085

This PR introduces proper handling for daily/weekly/monthly quota exhaustion errors:
1. Detects periodic usage limits and classifies them as `"quota_exhausted"` (rather than transient `rate_limit`).
2. Routes `quota_exhausted` through the same persistent backoff lane as billing failures (bypassing the provider for 5-24 hours).
3. Adds a new `agent:provider_tripped` internal hook event whenever a provider enters the disabled lane, allowing plugins (like ContextClaw) to observe and react to provider death.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenClaw previously treated periodic quota-exhaustion messages as ordinary transient rate limits, so a provider that had exhausted a daily/weekly/monthly usage window could keep being retried through the short rate-limit lane. This patch classifies those cases as `quota_exhausted`, routes them through the long disabled-lane backoff, and emits `agent:provider_tripped` when a provider enters that disabled lane.

Real environment tested:
- Branch/head: `feat/provider-circuit-breaker` at `6f2493db9043f43a30a0b59def58a9d37206a222`, then lint-only follow-up applied locally before push.
- Runtime path: real OpenClaw agent/auth-profile/failover modules from this branch, run through the repository's Node/Vitest project harness.
- Before-patch incident context: ScientificProgrammer reported a real OpenClaw deployment where a Gemini monthly spending-cap condition produced repeated `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` cascades across 26 runIds and about 390 exhausted-provider events.

Exact steps or command run after the patch:
- Ran `npm run format:check -- src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts`.
- Ran `pnpm lint --threads=8`.
- Ran `pnpm test src/agents/failover-error.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: Copied local command output from the after-patch run:

```text
format:check src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts: passed
pnpm lint --threads=8: passed
focused runtime-path suite:
  src/agents/failover-error.test.ts
  src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts
  src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts
  src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts
result: 314 assertions passed
```

Observed result after fix: The quota-exhaustion classifier lets quota text win over generic HTTP 429 rate-limit handling, active quota-disabled providers resolve as `quota_exhausted`, the 30-second billing recovery probe is not reused for quota exhaustion, and quota-disabled providers only probe near disabled-window expiry. The `agent:provider_tripped` hook dispatch path now satisfies the internal hook contract and narrows async dispatch errors safely.

What was not tested: I did not retest against a live already-exhausted Gemini billing account after this patch. The real-world production incident is included as before-patch validation of the cascade shape; the after-patch evidence is local OpenClaw runtime-path evidence.

target.title: Docker: add Mac migration and keep-awake helpers
target.body:
## What
Add operator helpers for Mac-hosted Docker deployments.

## Why
Running OpenClaw in Docker on macOS is easier to operate if the repo includes a simple host-awake helper and a low-disruption backup/restore path for moving from an Intel Mac to Apple Silicon.

## Included
- add `scripts/openclaw-keepawake.sh` to keep the host awake during long-running gateway sessions
- add `scripts/migrate/backup-openclaw.sh` and `scripts/migrate/restore-openclaw.sh` for migration backups
- preserve config, workspace, auth-profile secret-key state, repo `.env`, and Docker setup files
- harden backup archive and checksum file permissions and verify the external archive checksum before extraction
- add a focused migration-helper regression test and Docker docs

## Notes
This PR is intentionally separate from the Docker runtime/image PR so runtime behavior and host-operator helpers can be reviewed independently.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Mac-hosted Docker operator helpers can preserve OpenClaw config, workspace scripts, and auth-profile secret-key state during host migration, and can keep the host awake during long-running gateway sessions.
- **Real environment tested**: Intel macOS host with Docker Desktop and the existing OpenClaw Docker deployment; plus a local restore fixture that exercises the backup/restore scripts without touching the live config.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `bash -n scripts/migrate/backup-openclaw.sh scripts/migrate/restore-openclaw.sh scripts/migrate/lib.sh scripts/openclaw-keepawake.sh`; `pnpm test test/scripts/openclaw-migrate-helpers.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`; `pnpm docs:list`; `node scripts/check-no-conflict-markers.mjs`; `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD`.
- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
bash syntax ok

pnpm test test/scripts/openclaw-migrate-helpers.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
✓ openclaw Docker migration helpers > backs up and restores config, workspace, auth-profile secrets, and archive permissions
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard

docs:list ok (1313 lines)
check-no-conflict-markers passed
git diff --check passed
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The migration test creates a real archive, verifies the companion `.sha256`, proves the archive/checksum are owner-only, restores config/workspace/auth-profile secret files, and verifies restored `.env` permissions. The earlier live Docker proof also confirmed the backup preserves the digest and portfolio workspace scripts used by the deployed gateway.
- **What was not tested**: I did not run a destructive restore over the active live config on the same machine; the restore proof uses an isolated temp fixture so the live Docker deployment is not disturbed.

target.title: Persist hosted catalog snapshots in state
target.body:
<!-- hosted-feed-stack:start -->
## Hosted Marketplace Feed Stack

This is PR4 in the hosted marketplace feed stack. The stack should land in order, with maintainer review at each step before the later PRs wire hosted marketplace data into broader user flows.

1. #95846: refactor the bundled external plugin catalog toward the feed-shaped data model. Merged.
2. #95868: add the lazy hosted marketplace feed loader for the configured public feed URL, with guarded HTTPS fetch, checksum, schema, size, timeout, and bundled fallback behavior. Merged.
3. #95877: add accepted hosted snapshot fallback so a failed or unchanged hosted fetch can reuse the last valid feed payload. Merged.
4. #95964: persist hosted marketplace feed snapshots in OpenClaw state. This PR.
5. #95969: add marketplace source profile validation for hosted feed install candidates.
6. #95981: add configurable `marketplaces.feeds` and `marketplaces.sources` profiles.
7. #96155: add `openclaw plugins marketplace refresh` as the explicit manual snapshot refresh path.
8. #96158: add `openclaw plugins marketplace entries` as the explicit manual read path over hosted, snapshot, or bundled fallback entries.
9. #96194: add marketplace feed telemetry.

The broader follow-up after this stack is to decide whether and how marketplace entries should feed install/search/startup behavior, add automatic conditional refresh using `ETag` / `Last-Modified`, or move to ClawHub producer-side validation for `/v1/feeds/plugins` and `/v1/feeds/skills`.
<!-- hosted-feed-stack:end -->

## Stack Context

This is step 4 in the hosted feed rollout. It has been restacked directly on current `main` after #95846, #95868, and #95877 merged, so the diff is limited to the persistent snapshot storage layer and its tests.

## RFC Alignment

This PR is the persistent snapshot plumbing for the hosted catalog loader. It implements the SQLite storage part of RFC #19 rollout item 6: store the latest accepted hosted feed snapshot in OpenClaw state so the client can keep using a last-known-good catalog during HTTP 304 responses or transient hosted-feed failures.

It intentionally does not claim the full lifecycle refresh implementation. Named feed/source profiles, source-ref validation, signed envelopes, scheduled refresh, `maxStale`, and feed freshness reporting are still follow-up PRs.

## Maintainer Boundary Decision

Maintainer review accepts this PR as the durable cache layer only: persisted hosted feed bodies are inert snapshot material, not trusted install, search, startup, or source authority. Later stack PRs must explicitly validate source profiles and feed trust rules before consuming persisted entries for marketplace behavior.

The default shared-state SQLite store is intentional for hosted loader resilience after HTTP 304 or transient feed failures. `snapshotStore: null` remains the explicit opt-out for callers and tests that must avoid persistence.

## Summary
- Adds a SQLite-backed snapshot store for hosted official external plugin catalog responses in OpenClaw state.
- Makes the hosted feed loader use that state-backed store by default while preserving `snapshotStore: null` for callers and tests that need persistence disabled.
- Persists the feed body plus URL, status, ETag, Last-Modified, locally computed checksum, save time, and update time.
- Lets HTTP 304 and transient hosted feed failures use the last known good state snapshot before falling back to the bundled catalog.
- Keeps snapshots keyed by the resolved feed URL, which leaves room for later named feed/source profile work without mixing feeds.

## Deliberately Not Included

- Named local feed/source profile configuration.
- `sourceRef` validation against configured source profiles.
- Authenticated feed URLs or secret-reference resolution.
- Signed feed envelopes or trust-key configuration.
- Background refresh scheduling, `maxStale`, or stale-catalog status reporting.
- Search, install UX, skills, or telemetry.

Those remain follow-up steps after the core fallback storage path is reviewable.

## Validation

- `pnpm exec oxlint --deny-warnings src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog-snapshot-store.ts src/state/openclaw-state-schema.generated.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog-snapshot-store.ts src/state/openclaw-state-schema.generated.ts src/state/openclaw-state-db.generated.d.ts`
- pnpm db:kysely:check`r
- pnpm lint:kysely`r
- pnpm check:database-first-legacy-stores`r
- pnpm check:architecture`r
- pnpm tsgo:core`r
- `git diff --check`
- `CI=1 node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/plugins/official-external-plugin-catalog.test.ts` - 35 tests passed
- `codex review --base origin/main` - no actionable correctness issues found

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
This PR persists accepted hosted marketplace feed snapshots in OpenClaw SQLite state so the hosted catalog loader can reuse a last-known-good payload after live HTTP 304 responses or transient hosted-feed failures.

Real environment tested:
Windows host, `C:\src\openclaw-prflow\.worktrees\pr-95964`, source harness on commit `1f950286cd`, Node with `tsx`, isolated state directory `C:\\tmp\\openclaw-feeds-proof\\pr95964-live-snapshot-kysely`, and the live ClawHub feed endpoint `https://clawhub.ai/v1/feeds/plugins`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Removed the isolated state directory.
2. Ran a source harness that called `loadHostedOfficialExternalPluginCatalogEntries({ stateDir, timeoutMs: 30000, chunkTimeoutMs: 30000 })` against the live ClawHub feed.
3. Re-ran the same loader with `ifNoneMatch` set to the live ETag returned by step 2.
4. Read the persisted snapshot through `createSqliteHostedOfficialExternalPluginCatalogSnapshotStore({ stateDir })` and checked `state/openclaw.sqlite` on disk.

Evidence after fix:
The live harness printed:

```json
{
  "repoHead": "1f950286cd",
  "stateDir": "C:/tmp/openclaw-feeds-proof/pr95964-live-snapshot-kysely",
  "sqlitePath": "C:\\\\tmp\\\\openclaw-feeds-proof\\\\pr95964-live-snapshot-kysely\\\\state\\\\openclaw.sqlite",
  "sqliteBytes": 4096,
  "firstLoad": {
    "source": "hosted",
    "feedId": "clawhub-official",
    "schemaVersion": 1,
    "sequence": 16,
    "entries": 59,
    "etag": "\"sha256:cf3d85c1d6ede1907ca265e9b214ac6ff1c715c90c30ec65e8017addb590e32d-gzip\"",
    "lastModified": "Sat, 27 Jun 2026 18:43:36 GMT",
    "checksum": "sha256:cf3d85c1d6ede1907ca265e9b214ac6ff1c715c90c30ec65e8017addb590e32d"
  },
  "persistedSnapshot": {
    "feedUrl": "https://clawhub.ai/v1/feeds/plugins",
    "status": 200,
    "checksum": "sha256:cf3d85c1d6ede1907ca265e9b214ac6ff1c715c90c30ec65e8017addb590e32d",
    "savedAt": "2026-06-28T00:08:40.573Z",
    "bodyBytes": 21942
  },
  "secondConditionalLoad": {
    "source": "hosted-snapshot",
    "entries": 59,
    "feedId": "clawhub-official",
    "sequence": 16,
    "error": "hosted catalog feed returned HTTP 304",
    "status": 200,
    "etag": "\"sha256:cf3d85c1d6ede1907ca265e9b214ac6ff1c715c90c30ec65e8017addb590e32d-gzip\""
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix:
The first live load fetched the hosted feed and created the SQLite state database. The second conditional load received HTTP 304 from ClawHub and returned `source: "hosted-snapshot"` with the same 59 entries from the persisted SQLite snapshot.

What was not tested:
This proof did not exercise a packaged OpenClaw binary or CLI command path. The source harness covered the hosted loader's default persistent store wiring, and the focused tests cover snapshot validation, opt-out behavior, schema shape, and transient failure fallback.

target.title: Reject delete-after-run recurring cron jobs
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`deleteAfterRun` is a one-shot lifecycle flag, but cron accepted jobs that combined it with recurring schedules. That leaves the scheduler with conflicting semantics: a job can be marked for deletion after a run while also being scheduled to run repeatedly.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds service-level validation so `deleteAfterRun: true` is only accepted for `schedule.kind="at"` on new jobs and on updates that would newly create or re-enable the invalid combination.

The update path is intentionally compatibility-aware for already-persisted legacy jobs: an existing recurring `deleteAfterRun` job can still be renamed, disabled, or cleared with `deleteAfterRun: false`. A disabled legacy-invalid job cannot be re-enabled until the flag is cleared or the schedule is changed to `at`.

## User Impact

Valid recurring jobs are unchanged. One-shot jobs still default to `deleteAfterRun`. Invalid new recurring delete-after-run jobs fail fast with a clear validation error instead of being persisted as scheduler state.

Users who already have an invalid persisted recurring job are not trapped: they can disable it, rename it, or clear `deleteAfterRun`; re-enabling is allowed after the job is valid again.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.jobs.test.ts` — 71 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts` — 42 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts -t "delete-after-run"` — 3 passed, 18 skipped
- `node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check src/cron/service/jobs.ts src/cron/service.jobs.test.ts src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts`
- `node_modules/oxlint/bin/oxlint src/cron/service/jobs.ts src/cron/service.jobs.test.ts src/gateway/server.cron.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

Redacted live Gateway handler proof from a temp state directory against this branch:

```text
gateway add recurring deleteAfterRun: error cron deleteAfterRun is only supported for schedule.kind="at"
gateway rename legacy invalid: ok {"id":"legacy-delete-after-run-recurring","name":"renamed legacy invalid","enabled":true,"deleteAfterRun":true,"scheduleKind":"every"}
gateway disable legacy invalid: ok {"id":"legacy-delete-after-run-recurring","name":"renamed legacy invalid","enabled":false,"deleteAfterRun":true,"scheduleKind":"every"}
gateway reenable before clear: error invalid cron.update params: cron deleteAfterRun is only supported for schedule.kind="at"
gateway clear legacy flag: ok {"id":"legacy-delete-after-run-recurring","name":"renamed legacy invalid","enabled":false,"deleteAfterRun":false,"scheduleKind":"every"}
gateway reenable after clear: ok {"id":"legacy-delete-after-run-recurring","name":"renamed legacy invalid","enabled":true,"deleteAfterRun":false,"scheduleKind":"every"}
```

target.title: feat(gateway): wire coding tools into /tools/invoke HTTP surface
target.body:
> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `4fe6f873c9` (scope owner gate to built-in host-FS write tools; restacked on #85664 `d625c6de23`).** Mirrors #85664's read-side fix for `write`/`edit` and addresses the ClawSweeper plugin-fallback finding (`docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` write section). Restacked onto #85664's new base `d625c6de23` (which carries the read-side scoping); the write-side delta on top scopes the overstated "refused even when both config keys are set" wording to the **built-in** `write`/`edit` tools across the `hostFsWrite` config help (`src/config/schema.help.ts`), the `hostFsWrite` member comment (`src/config/types.gateway.ts`), the `hostFsWrite` bullet in `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`, and the write section of `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` (prose, truth table, _Plugin name collision_ note). Per the resolver (`src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts`): for a non-owner caller the built-in is never materialized, but a same-named allowlisted plugin tool still resolves under the normal `gateway.tools.allow`/`deny` policy. Help strings / type comments / docs only — **no runtime code changed** (verified: the write-side commit `27deceb882..4fe6f873c9` touches only `src/config/schema.help.ts`, `src/config/types.gateway.ts`, and the two `docs/gateway/*.md` files — no `src/gateway/`, `src/security/`, `zod-schema.ts`, or `schema.labels.ts`), so the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. Verified in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` linux container: `pnpm check:docs` (format clean, markdownlint 0 errors, MDX ok, 0 broken links) and `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts` (23/23) both green.

Builds on #85664. Narrow extension of the broader direct-invoke umbrella tracked in #37131.

## Summary

Extends #85664's narrow read-only landing to also expose `write` and `edit` over the gateway direct-invoke surfaces (HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK RPC `tools.invoke`, which share the `resolveGatewayScopedTools` resolver). Same **triple-key owner-gated** shape as #85664's `read`: a surface-specific class opt-in **plus** the per-tool name in `gateway.tools.allow` **plus** `senderIsOwner === true`, all on the `http` surface.

## Re-stacked on #85664's owner-gated read base (head `afe36ccf456f`, base upstream `023ce6e96c38`)

This branch was rebuilt on top of #85664's current head `5ca72af1ba` (itself rebased onto current upstream `023ce6e96c38`) and the write/edit extension re-applied with the **same owner-gate**. This directly resolves the prior ClawSweeper [P1].

**Bot [P1] (resolved):** upstream commit `02182d5a3031` ("refactor: remove sender owner tool gating") deleted the `ownerOnly` enforcement helper, so the earlier `ownerOnly: true` metadata tag this PR added became dead code — a trusted-proxy `operator.write` caller could have reached an allowlisted mutating tool with no owner check. **Fix:** this PR no longer relies on that tag. `write`/`edit` are materialized into the direct-invoke candidate set **only when the resolved sender is owner** (`senderIsOwner === true`, evaluated at resolve time in `tool-resolution.ts`), exactly mirroring #85664's read gate. A trusted-proxy `operator.write` caller is now refused even with both config keys set (proven below, cases 2 & 4).

## Triple-key gating

To reach a write-class tool on the direct-invoke surface, ALL must hold:

| Key | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsWrite: true` | Class-level opt-in for the whole write family. | `false` |
| `gateway.tools.allow: [...]` includes the tool name | Per-tool enablement. | not present |
| `senderIsOwner === true` | Caller resolves to owner/admin (trusted-proxy `operator.admin`, or shared-secret bearer). | — |

Truth table (tool name `T` ∈ `{write, edit}`, surface = `http`):

| `allow` ∋ `T` | `hostFsWrite` | sender owner | `T` reachable |
|---|---|---|---|
| no  | no  | —   | ❌ |
| yes | no  | yes | ❌ (class opt-in off; built-in not materialized; `dangerous_allow` audit fires) |
| no  | yes | yes | ❌ (not in allow) |
| yes | yes | **no**  | ❌ (**owner gate** — refused) |
| yes | yes | yes | ✅ |

## Scope

- **Adds:** `write`, `edit` to direct-invoke (behind `hostFsWrite: true` + allow + owner).
- **Inherits from #85664:** `read` via `hostFsRead: true` + allow + owner.
- **`apply_patch`:** present in `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` for defense-in-depth, but the coding-tools factory produces no `apply_patch` entry, so it is never materialized (proven below, case 7).
- **NOT exposed:** `exec`, `process`, `spawn`, `shell` — RCE-class; out of scope for this PR.

## Audit

- `gateway.tools_invoke_http.host_write_allow` fires only when BOTH `hostFsWrite: true` AND a write-class name is in `allow` (mirrors `host_read_allow`); escalates to `critical` on non-loopback bind.
- `dangerous_allow` suppression for `write`/`edit` is **source-aware** — suppressed only when the `hostFsWrite` class opt-in is active; `apply_patch` is never suppressed.

## Files changed

- `src/config/types.gateway.ts` — adds `hostFsWrite?: boolean` to `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns`
- `src/config/zod-schema.ts` — `hostFsWrite` schema entry
- `src/config/schema.help.ts`, `src/config/schema.labels.ts` — field help + label
- `src/security/dangerous-tools.ts` — adds `"write"`, `"edit"` to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY`
- `src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts` — owner-gated write/edit materialization; coding built-ins win plugin name collision when class opt-in active
- `src/security/audit-gateway-config.ts` — `host_write_allow` finding; source-aware `dangerous_allow` suppression
- `src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts` — write coding-tool collision precedence (owner→built-in, non-owner→plugin, opt-in-off→plugin)
- `src/security/audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` — `host_write_allow` dual-key + source-aware `dangerous_allow` suppression cases (incl. `apply_patch` never suppressed)
- `src/agents/agent-tools.ts` — coding-tool factory plumbing
- `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`, `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md`, `docs/gateway/security/audit-checks.md` — `hostFsWrite` opt-in, triple-key truth tables, `host_write_allow` catalog row

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Extend #85664's owner-gated direct-invoke `read` to also expose `write` and `edit` over the Gateway HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` and SDK `tools.invoke` surfaces, behind the same triple key (class opt-in `directInvoke.hostFsWrite` + per-tool `gateway.tools.allow` + `senderIsOwner`). Re-stacked on #85664's owner-gated read base so write/edit materialize only for an owner sender — resolving the prior [P1] where upstream `02182d5a3031` made the old `ownerOnly` tag dead code.

**Real environment tested:** Built fresh from head `afe36ccf456f` inside a `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64-native container (full runtime build → `dist/index.js`; SDK → `packages/sdk/dist/index.mjs`). Each case boots a live `openclaw gateway run` on a real loopback socket; owner = trusted-proxy `operator.admin` (and a shared-secret bearer token for the SDK case), non-owner = trusted-proxy `operator.write`. Target file states are re-read from disk after each call to prove real host mutation or its absence.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:** `PROOF_HEAD=afe36ccf456f bash .proof-63919.sh` inside the container — boots nine gateway configs and dispatches `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` over HTTP `/tools/invoke` and SDK `tools.invoke`, asserting the HTTP status and re-reading each target file from disk.

**Evidence after fix:** live terminal output captured from the container at head `afe36ccf456f`:

```
=== HEAD: afe36ccf456f ===

### LIVE PROOF (HTTP trusted-proxy surface) ###

--- 1 W-OWNER-ENABLED (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully wrote 24 bytes to /work/.proof63/w1.txt"}]}}
  file=OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f
--- 2 W-NONOWNER-DENIED (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, operator.write, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.write] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=PRESEED
--- 3 E-OWNER-ENABLED (allow=[edit] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=edit) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully replaced 1 block(s) in /work/.proof63/e3.txt."}],"details":{"diff":" 1 ALPHA\\n-2 BETA\\n+2 GAMMA-afe36ccf456f", ...}}}
  file=ALPHA / GAMMA-afe36ccf456f
--- 4 E-NONOWNER-DENIED (allow=[edit] hostFsWrite=true, operator.write, tool=edit) ---
  [HTTP operator.write] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: edit"}}
  file=ALPHA / BETA  (unchanged)
--- 5 W-DENIED-no-opt-in (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=false, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=<absent>
--- 6 W-DENIED-no-allow (allow=[] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=<absent>
--- 7 APPLYPATCH-denied (allow=[apply_patch] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=apply_patch) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: apply_patch"}}
--- 8 READ-REGRESSION (allow=[read] hostFsRead=true, operator.admin, tool=read) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"READ-BASE-OK-afe36ccf456f\\n"}]}}

### LIVE PROOF (SDK token surface — shared-secret bearer = owner) ###

--- 9 SDK-W-OWNER (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, token bearer, tool=write) ---
  [SDK token] {"ok":true,"toolName":"write","output":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully wrote 28 bytes to /work/.proof63/w9.txt"}]},"source":"core"}
  file=SDK-OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f

=== PROOF COMPLETE: HEAD=afe36ccf456f ===
```

Independent re-read of persisted file states after the run: `w1.txt`=`OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f`, `w2.txt`=`PRESEED` (non-owner write left it untouched), `e3.txt`=`ALPHA/GAMMA-afe36ccf456f`, `e4.txt`=`ALPHA/BETA` (non-owner edit left it untouched), `w5.txt` and `w6.txt` absent (never created), `w9.txt`=`SDK-OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f`.

**Observed result after fix:** All nine cases behaved as specified — owner writes/edits land and mutate the host file; a non-owner `operator.write` caller is refused `write`/`edit` (404) with the target file left untouched; missing class opt-in or missing allow entry both deny; `apply_patch` is never materialized; and the inherited `read` path still works:

| # | Case | Config | Caller | HTTP/SDK | File state |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | write owner | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 200 wrote 24 bytes | written |
| 2 | write **non-owner** | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.write` | 404 not_found | **unchanged** (owner gate) |
| 3 | edit owner | allow=[edit], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 200 replaced 1 block | edited |
| 4 | edit **non-owner** | allow=[edit], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.write` | 404 not_found | **unchanged** (owner gate) |
| 5 | write no opt-in | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=false | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | never created |

| 7 | apply_patch | allow=[apply_patch], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | factory never materializes it |
| 8 | read regression | allow=[read], hostFsRead=true | `operator.admin` | 200 + contents | #85664 base intact |
| 9 | write owner (SDK) | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | token bearer | ok=true wrote 28 bytes | written |

Supplemental (not a substitute for the live proof above), all in the same `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64 container at head `afe36ccf456f`: unit tests — **160 passed** across the gateway test projects (`tools-invoke-http` 42 + `tool-resolution` 5 + `audit-gateway-tools-http` 19, ×3 projects = 7 file-projects); `lint` (oxlint, 0/0), `tsgo:core` (0), `check:test-types` (0), and `plugin-sdk-surface-report` vitest (4/4). **`checks-node-core-fast` / plugin-SDK surface budget:** this PR adds `hostFsWrite?` as a *field* on the existing `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` type — **zero new top-level exports** — so `plugin-sdk-surface-report --check` passes on the `+1` budget already carried by #85664's base commit (`config-types` 416 / public 10285 / public-fn 5164 / deprecated 3238). No further budget change in this PR.

**Not tested:** `exec`, `process`, `spawn`, `shell` (RCE-class; out of scope for this PR and never exposed by the direct-invoke factory).

target.title: fix: accept valid MCP tool schemas without strict inputSchema.type check
target.body:
## Summary
Fixes MCP server loading failure when tools have `inputSchema` without explicit `type: "object"`.

Closes #63602

## Changes
- `listAllTools` validates each `tools/list` page **once** with `RelaxedListToolsResultSchema` and catalogs **that same validated payload** — there is no strict-then-relist flow, so a stateful or hostile server cannot pass validation with one response and have a different response consumed (no validation-to-use gap).
- `RelaxedToolSchema` is **derived from the pinned SDK's own `ToolSchema`** (`ToolSchema.extend` / `ToolSchema.shape.inputSchema.extend`) and differs from it at exactly one point: the root `inputSchema.type` is an optional string instead of `z.literal("object")`. Every other field — `outputSchema`, task `execution` metadata, `annotations`, pagination — keeps the exact pinned SDK contract, so malformed metadata still rejects the page.
- After every page parse, the SDK's `cacheToolMetadata()` is invoked with the validated tools — the same side effect `client.listTools()` runs — so `callTool()` enforces output-schema validation and task-required guards identically to the SDK flow. The call is optional (no crash if a future SDK removes the method) and a dedicated test pins the method's existence so a dependency upgrade that drops it fails loudly rather than silently shedding the guards.
- `normalizeInputSchema()` backfills `type: "object"` only when the field is missing; `isValidRootInputSchema()` + the catalog gate skip tools with an explicit non-object root type (e.g. `"string"`, `"array"`) with a `logWarn`. Server `toolCount` reports accepted tools only.

## Root Cause
The MCP SDK v1.29.0 `ToolSchema` validates `inputSchema.type` as `z.literal("object")`. Some MCP servers (especially older SDKs or custom implementations) omit `type` from `inputSchema`, returning `{ properties: { ... } }`, which trips a Zod validation error: `invalid_value expected "object" in inputSchema.type` and crashes the whole catalog.

## v6 notes (current revision)
Addresses the P1 from the latest review (validation-to-use gap, confidence 0.96) by adopting its recommended repair: single-response validation that differs from the pinned SDK `ToolSchema` only at the root `inputSchema.type`, with SDK-equivalent metadata caching, explicit non-object filtering, and pagination preserved.
- The strict-then-relist flow is gone entirely; the request count per page is 1 in all cases.
- New stateful-server regression: a server that returns a benign typeless payload on request 1 and a malicious payload on request 2 gets exactly one `tools/list` request, and only the validated payload is cataloged. **Mutation-checked**: against the previous strict-then-relist implementation this test fails with the catalog containing the drifted (never-strictly-validated) tool — reproducing the reported attack — and the server log showing two list requests.
- New SDK-seam pin test: `Client.prototype.cacheToolMetadata` must exist, making the documented private-method dependency an explicit, loudly-failing audit point on SDK upgrades (P1 risk #2 from the same review).
- Relaxation-scope unit tests: malformed `outputSchema`, malformed `execution.taskSupport`, and missing `inputSchema` all still reject the page; pagination (`nextCursor`) keeps the SDK contract.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: Bundle MCP tool cataloging accepts tools whose root `inputSchema` omits `type` by validating each `tools/list` response once against a schema derived from the pinned SDK contract (relaxed only at the root `inputSchema.type`), backfills `type: "object"`, skips explicit non-object roots, keeps servers with unrelated strict failures rejected, never issues a second list request, and preserves the SDK's `cacheToolMetadata()` side effects for every page.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS from the rebased PR head, Node 25.2.1, pnpm 11.2.2, driving the real bundle MCP runtime (`getOrCreateSessionMcpRuntime` → `getCatalog()` → `callTool()`) against four real out-of-process stdio MCP servers, one of them stateful (different payload per request).

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran `pnpm test src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`, `pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:core:test`, `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`, and `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1` on both files, plus a live four-server e2e script via `pnpm exec tsx`.

Evidence after fix:
```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  42 passed (42)
pnpm tsgo:core / tsgo:core:test: clean
oxlint / oxfmt --check: clean

e2e (four live stdio servers):
tools: ["legacySdkServer/search_docs","driftServer/benign_typeless_tool"]
servers: {"legacySdkServer":{"toolCount":1},"driftServer":{"toolCount":1}}
rejected: ["badOutputServer","mixedServer"]
driftServer tools/list requests received: 1        // exactly one — no second-request window
callTool(search_docs) → McpError -32600: Tool search_docs has an output schema but did not
  return structured content                        // metadata cache populated from the same response
```

Observed result after fix: A `tools/list` payload whose `inputSchema` omits `type` catalogs cleanly with `type: "object"` backfilled; an explicit `type: "array"` sibling is skipped with a logWarn; servers with malformed `outputSchema` (alone or mixed with a repairable tool) stay rejected; a stateful server attempting response drift receives exactly one list request and only its validated payload is cataloged; `callTool()` enforces `outputSchema` for typeless tools exactly as the SDK strict flow would.

What was not tested: A live third-party MCP server connected to an actual model-provider tool call was not tested in this environment.

target.title: feat(plugins): add session followup turn API and gateway-restart extension
target.body:
## Summary

Add a new `runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn()` method to the plugin runtime, enabling plugins to schedule proactive agent turns for any session — including cold sessions with no active user interaction.

> **Supersedes #60951** — rebased onto latest main, all CI issues resolved, bug fixes included.

## Problem

Plugins have no way to inject a user-role message into an existing session and trigger an agent turn. The available primitives (`enqueueSystemEvent`, `subagent.run`) either don't initiate turns or create new sessions instead of continuing the original conversation. This blocks use cases like:

- **Post-restart session resume** — an agent restarts its own gateway and needs to automatically continue working after the restart completes
- **Model backend switching** — an agent swaps inference backends (e.g., switching between local LLMs on a single-GPU workstation) and needs to resume the session on the new model with full context
- **Proactive plugin notifications** — a service detects an external event and needs to wake a sleeping session
- **Deferred task completion** — a plugin schedules follow-up work after a long-running background operation

## Solution

### Core API: `runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn()`

A new `followup` namespace on `PluginRuntimeCore` that:

1. **Reconstructs a full `FollowupRun`** from the session store via `buildFollowupRunForSession()` — resolving agent identity, workspace, provider/model, session file, and delivery context from the persisted `SessionEntry`.

2. **Enqueues the run** into the existing followup queue infrastructure with preserved queue mode (respects `mode` of any existing queue for the session key, defaults to `"followup"`).

3. **Handles cold-start drain** by detecting whether the session's followup queue is already draining. If not (cold session), creates a fresh followup runner with preloaded session metadata and a no-op typing controller, then kicks the drain loop.

```typescript
// Plugin usage
const enqueued = await api.runtime.followup.enqueueFollowupTurn({
  sessionKey: "agent:my-agent:telegram:direct:12345",
  prompt: "Background task complete. Here are the results...",
  source: "my-plugin",
});
```

### Bundled Extension: `gateway-restart`

A reference implementation that demonstrates the followup turn API:

- **Tool** (`gateway_restart`): Agent calls this to run optional allowlisted pre-commands, write a restart marker (only after commands succeed), then exit the process. Systemd (or equivalent) auto-restarts the gateway.

- **Service** (`gateway-restart-watcher`): On startup, checks for the restart marker. If found, calls `enqueueFollowupTurn()` to resume the original session with a completion message. Marker is deleted only after successful enqueue — preserved for retry on failure.

Disabled by default (`enabledByDefault: false`). Opt-in via `plugins.entries.gateway-restart.enabled: true`.

## Files Changed

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/build-followup-run.ts` | New: cold-session FollowupRun builder with session store resolution |
| `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-followup.ts` | New: plugin runtime followup namespace with hot/cold session detection |
| `src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts` | Add `followup` type to `PluginRuntimeCore` |
| `src/plugins/runtime/index.ts` | Wire `createRuntimeFollowup()` |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/index.ts` | New: gateway-restart plugin (tool + watcher service) |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/openclaw.plugin.json` | New: plugin manifest |
| `extensions/gateway-restart/package.json` | New: package metadata |
| `test/helpers/plugins/plugin-runtime-mock.ts` | Add `followup` to mock |
| `**/**.test.ts` | 24 unit tests across 3 test files |

## Bug Fixes (vs #60951)

1. **Marker written after pre-commands** — prevents stale markers when a pre-command fails
2. **Marker deleted after enqueue confirmed** — preserves marker for retry if `enqueueFollowupTurn()` returns false
3. **Hot session guard** — `rememberFollowupDrainCallback` only registers for cold sessions; existing hot-session drain runners are not overwritten
4. **Queue mode preservation** — `enqueueFollowupRun` respects existing queue mode instead of forcing `"followup"`
5. **Session metadata preload** — cold followup runners receive preloaded session store and entry for accounting/refresh paths

## Testing

- 24 unit tests across 3 test files (all passing)
- `pnpm check` passes (tsgo + oxlint + all architecture checks)
- Rebased onto latest main
- End-to-end verified on a local workstation running OpenClaw 2026.4.8 with Gemma 4 26B via llama.cpp

## Use Cases This Enables

This API is the foundation for several plugins currently in development:

- **`model-switch`** — seamless switching between local LLMs on single-GPU workstations, with session continuation via `enqueueFollowupTurn()` after the new model is healthy
- **`inference-guard`** — priority-aware request scheduling for single-slot inference (heartbeat/cron defer behind user messages)
- **Self-modifying agents** — agents that update their own config, restart the gateway, and validate changes automatically

## Test plan

- [ ] `pnpm check` passes
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run extensions/gateway-restart/index.test.ts` — 6 tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run src/plugins/runtime/runtime-followup.test.ts` — 9 tests pass
- [ ] `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/queue/build-followup-run.test.ts` — 9 tests pass
- [ ] Gateway-restart tool writes marker only after pre-commands succeed
- [ ] Watcher service enqueues followup turn and deletes marker on success
- [ ] Watcher preserves marker on enqueue failure

## AI Disclosure

This PR was developed with AI assistance (Claude Code). Testing level: **fully tested** — 24 unit tests, `pnpm check` green, end-to-end verified on local hardware. The author understands all code in this PR and can explain design decisions.

target.title: Add Swedish control UI locale
target.body:
## Summary
- add Swedish (`sv`) as a supported control UI locale
- generate and register `ui/src/i18n/locales/sv.ts` against the current 1118-key source locale set
- preserve the newer `ar`, `it`, `vi`, `nl`, and `fa` locale registrations while syncing metadata and language labels
- update registry tests for the expanded locale list and Swedish browser locale detection, including `sv-SE`

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Swedish browser users with `sv` or `sv-SE` now resolve to the Swedish Control UI locale, and the lazy locale registry can load the real Swedish translation module.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `feat/control-ui-swedish-locale` at `cd3be8c14b`, rebased onto upstream `main` (`9e4eca00ff`), running Node v22.22.2 with repo dependencies installed by pnpm.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Imported the actual Control UI registry and Swedish locale module from the checked-out branch and resolved Swedish browser language values without mocks:

```console
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { resolveNavigatorLocale, loadLazyLocaleTranslation, SUPPORTED_LOCALES } from "./ui/src/i18n/lib/registry.ts"; const sv=await loadLazyLocaleTranslation("sv"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ hasSv: SUPPORTED_LOCALES.includes("sv"), svSE: resolveNavigatorLocale("sv-SE"), sv: resolveNavigatorLocale("sv"), label: sv?.languages?.sv, commonHealth: sv?.common?.health }, null, 2));'
```

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real local checkout:

```console
{
  "hasSv": true,
  "svSE": "sv",
  "sv": "sv",
  "label": "Svenska",
  "commonHealth": "Status"
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The runtime registry includes `sv`, `resolveNavigatorLocale("sv-SE")` returns `sv`, `resolveNavigatorLocale("sv")` returns `sv`, and the loaded Swedish locale exposes Swedish UI strings.

**What was not tested:** No known gaps for this registry/lazy-locale behavior. Full browser screenshot proof was not added because this change is in the locale registry and generated locale data rather than a visual layout path.

## Validation
- `pnpm ui:i18n:check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/i18n/registry.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- committed diff credential-pattern scan found no secret material

## Notes
- the Swedish locale now matches the current source key shape; untranslated newer strings are tracked as fallback keys by the existing i18n metadata flow

target.title: feat: Rust/GTK4 Linux companion app (#75, alternative track)
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a native Linux desktop companion under `apps/linux-gtk/` written in **Rust + GTK4 + libadwaita**
- Alternative-track contribution for [#75](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/75) alongside @tiagonix's C/GTK4 work at `apps/linux/` — different language, zero path conflicts, same gateway protocol
- Steady base, early. Needs security review, packaging, and UI polish

## Why another one

I started on a prototype using web-tech (Electron) before reading through #75, then saw [@steipete's comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/75#issuecomment-3747113141):

> My vision for this is a cute gtk-native Linux app. Esp for the statusbar features.

Switched tracks to native GTK. @tiagonix's C implementation was the reference, but I wanted to see what the Rust ecosystem gives us here (borrow-checked gateway client, fewer NULL pointer surprises, tokio for async RPC, `cargo test` for unit coverage).

Happy to fold it in, leave it as a community showcase, or archive it — whichever serves the project.

## What's included

**Library crate** (`crates/gateway-client/`, no GTK dependency):
- Async tokio + tungstenite WebSocket client with auto-reconnect
- Typed request/event frames (serde_json-backed)
- Device identity with ed25519 challenge signing
- Env > config-file > defaults resolution for URL / token / TLS
- 24 unit tests

**GTK4 app** (`crates/app/`):
- AdwOverlaySplitView main window with responsive sidebar (collapses < 600sp)
- Bridge layer pumping tokio events into GLib main loop via async-channel
- 17 views: Chat, Overview, Agents, Channels, Sessions, Skills, Usage, Cron, Retros, Workflows, Instances, Logs, Config, Settings, Debug, Control Room, About
- **Chat**: live send/receive with Markdown rendering, per-session model picker (`sessions.patch`), typing indicator, thinking/tool-call toggles, history via `chat.history`
- **Agents**: master/detail with editable settings (`agents.update`) and workspace files (`agents.files.list`)
- Snapshot refresh pattern: parallel `agents.list` / `sessions.list` / `models.list` / `channels.status` RPCs on connect
- 42 unit tests

**Supporting files**: .desktop, .metainfo.xml, SVG icon, CSS (orange accent + libadwaita theming), README with per-distro build instructions

## Known gaps

- **Security review needed** — device identity handling, TLS verification flow, token persistence
- No packaging yet (Flatpak/deb/AppImage) — build-from-source only
- Tool-call rendering is basic (JSON in collapsible blocks)
- No system tray on tiling WMs (StatusNotifierWatcher absent) — fails gracefully
- Config editor is read-only
- Polling timers could be replaced with event-driven updates

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo build --release` — zero warnings
- [x] `cargo test --release` — 66 tests pass (24 gateway-client + 42 app)
- [x] Smoke-tested against gateway v2026.4.3 on Arch Linux (bspwm/X11, Cairo renderer)
- [ ] Test on Wayland / GNOME Shell
- [ ] Test on Ubuntu 24.04 with stock GTK4/libadwaita packages
- [ ] Security audit of device identity + TLS flow

target.title: CI: report circular dependencies in PRs
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: We did not have a repo-standard way to detect and inspect runtime circular imports across `src`, `extensions`, and `scripts`.
- Why it matters: These cycles can drag large strongly connected components into the runtime graph and make lazy/runtime boundaries harder to preserve.
- What changed: I added `dependency-cruiser` repo tooling, a focused circular-dependency config, a local report script, and a non-blocking CI job that publishes PR summaries plus raw artifacts.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): I did not start failing CI on existing cycles and I did not refactor any of the current cycle clusters in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A
- Scenario the test should lock in: N/A
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: This is tooling/CI wiring, so the direct guardrail is running the report script and validating the emitted artifacts/summary locally.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: The value here is report generation rather than product behavior. I verified the report output directly.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

PRs now get a non-blocking `dependency-circular-report` job that publishes:

- a summary table of cycle-heavy scopes
- a sorted descending directory/module list
- a Mermaid visualization for scope distribution and largest SCC clusters
- uploaded artifacts with raw JSON and full cycle listings

Local development also gets:

- `pnpm deps:circular`
- `pnpm deps:circular:json`
- `pnpm deps:circular:report`

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR] -> [standard CI lanes only] -> [no circular-import visibility]

After:
[PR] -> [dependency-circular-report job] -> [step summary + artifacts]
     -> [sorted scopes/directories + SCC graphs]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: CI now runs `dependency-cruiser` against repo sources to generate reports. This is limited to checked-in code in the Actions workspace and does not expand runtime privileges beyond normal repo CI tooling.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js v25.8.1 local shell
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default repo config

### Steps

1. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular`
2. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular:report`
3. Inspect `.artifacts/dependency-cruiser/step-summary.md`, `report.md`, `summary.json`, and `cycle-violations.txt`

### Expected

- `dependency-cruiser` finds current runtime cycles using the repo config
- the report script emits a readable sorted summary plus raw artifacts
- CI can reuse the same script to publish a non-blocking PR report

### Actual

- Local runs completed and emitted the expected artifact set

- Top cycle-heavy scopes were `extensions/browser`, `src/agents`, `extensions/mattermost`, `extensions/zalouser`, and `src/cli`

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

## Human Verification (required)

- Edge cases checked: I confirmed type-only imports are excluded, test files are excluded, the report groups by scope and exact directory, and the summary stays non-blocking.

## Review Conversations

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

target.title: feat(plugins): upgrade llm_input/llm_output hooks from fire-and-forget to modifying
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `llm_input` and `llm_output` plugin hooks are fire-and-forget (observe-only). Plugins cannot block, modify, or redact LLM calls/responses.
- Why it matters: Plugin authors need the ability to intercept LLM traffic for compliance, content filtering, prompt rewriting, and response redaction use cases.
- What changed: Both hooks now run sequentially via `runModifyingHook`. `llm_input` can return `block`, `blockReason`, `prompt`, or `systemPrompt` overrides. `llm_output` can return replacement `assistantTexts`. The attempt runner awaits both hooks and applies returned modifications.
- Security hardening (follow-up commits):
  - `PluginBlockedError` sentinel prevents failover logic from misclassifying plugin blocks as transient provider failures (e.g. rate-limit/auth errors)
  - `allowPromptInjection=false` enforcement: `llm_input` hooks are constrained via `constrainLlmInputPromptInjectionHook` when the policy is set, matching existing `before_agent_start`/`before_prompt_build` behavior
  - Image re-detection after prompt rewrite with full rollback on failure (reverts prompt, systemPrompt, and images to pre-hook state)
  - Priority layering: higher-priority (earlier) plugins win for prompt/systemPrompt overrides (`acc ?? next` merge strategy); `evolveEvent` propagates effective state to lower-priority handlers
  - Payload cloning: `historyMessages` and `assistantTexts` are shallow-copied before passing to hooks to prevent in-place mutation bypassing the merge contract
  - `lastAssistant` stripping: when `llm_output` overrides `assistantTexts`, text/content/errorMessage are cleared from `lastAssistant` to prevent reasoning blocks and error metadata from leaking downstream, while preserving `stopReason` and `usage` for failover logic
  - Plugin-blocked errors are excluded from overflow and rate-limit heuristics in both `run.ts` and `agent-runner-execution.ts`
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Hook event shapes are unchanged (additive result types only). Plugins that return `void` behave identically to before. No changes to other hooks, plugin discovery, or the Plugin SDK public surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #57585 — adds a complementary `transform_context` hook at the context-window layer; this PR operates at the prompt-assembly/response-delivery layer instead
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Plugins registering `llm_input` hooks can now return `{ block: true, blockReason: "..." }` to prevent an LLM call from being made.
- Plugins registering `llm_input` hooks can return `{ prompt, systemPrompt }` to override the prompt/system prompt sent to the LLM.
- Plugins registering `llm_output` hooks can return `{ assistantTexts: [...] }` to replace the assistant response (e.g. for redaction).
- Existing plugins that return `void` from these hooks are unaffected — fully backward compatible.
- When `allowPromptInjection=false` is set for a plugin, `llm_input` prompt/systemPrompt fields are stripped from the hook result (block/blockReason still honored).

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[prompt ready] -> llm_input (fire-and-forget, no return) -> [LLM call] -> [response] -> llm_output (fire-and-forget) -> [delivered]

After:
[prompt ready] -> await llm_input -> [block? -> PluginBlockedError] or [apply prompt/systemPrompt overrides -> re-detect images] -> [LLM call] -> [response] -> await llm_output -> [apply assistantTexts override, strip lastAssistant] -> [delivered]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Plugins can now modify prompts and block/alter LLM responses. This is gated behind the existing plugin hook registration mechanism — only installed and enabled plugins can register these hooks. The blocking path throws a dedicated `PluginBlockedError` sentinel that is logged and excluded from failover/overflow misclassification. Prompt modifications are subject to `allowPromptInjection` policy enforcement. Hook payloads are cloned to prevent mutation. Response overrides strip original content from `lastAssistant` to prevent data leaks. Prompt/response modifications flow through the same trust boundary as existing modifying hooks (e.g. `before_tool_call`).

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / Bun
- Model/provider: Any
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): Plugin with `llm_input`/`llm_output` hook registered

### Steps

1. Register a plugin with an `llm_input` hook that returns `{ block: true, blockReason: "test" }`
2. Trigger an agent run
3. Observe the LLM call is blocked with the logged reason

### Expected

- LLM call is blocked and error is thrown with reason

### Actual

target.title: fix(sessions): remap stale same-agent paths into the current state dir
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: persisted absolute `sessionFile` paths for the same agent can survive a state-root move (for example `/home/rai/.openclaw/...` to `/Users/rai/.openclaw/...`) and still be treated as structurally valid.
- Why it matters: resumed or targeted session writes then try to create/write the old root path and fail before the agent can continue.
- What changed: same-agent cross-root absolute session paths are now remapped into the current state dir's `agents/<agent>/sessions/<file>` path, and a regression test locks that behavior in.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): cross-agent fallback behavior and unrelated absolute-path normalization logic were left intact.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #61321
- Related #61321
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: `resolvePathWithinSessionsDir()` preserved structurally valid absolute session paths even when they pointed at the same agent under an old state root.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression coverage for same-agent cross-root absolute `sessionFile` metadata.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the absolute-path normalization and sibling-agent fallback logic around this area was introduced in February 2026; local blame points at the existing fallback path in `src/config/sessions/paths.ts`.
- Why this regressed now: a machine carrying Linux-rooted persisted session metadata was later used on macOS, so the stale absolute path became invalid only after the root move.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: stale `HOME`, stale `OPENCLAW_HOME`, stale `session.store` override, and provider-specific causes were ruled out locally.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/config/sessions.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: a same-agent absolute `sessionFile` from an old root is remapped into the current state dir instead of being preserved.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the failure is in pure path-resolution logic, before session execution or provider behavior.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): nearby cross-root and cross-agent fallback tests cover adjacent logic but not this same-agent remap case.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Resumed or targeted sessions with stale same-agent absolute transcript paths are recovered into the current state dir instead of failing on the old root path.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
old absolute sessionFile (/home/rai/.../agents/shikamaru/sessions/sess-1.jsonl)
  -> treated as structurally valid
  -> write lock tries old root
  -> ENOENT on /home/rai

After:
old absolute sessionFile (/home/rai/.../agents/shikamaru/sessions/sess-1.jsonl)
  -> recognized as same-agent stale root
  -> remapped to current state dir agents/shikamaru/sessions/sess-1.jsonl
  -> write proceeds
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.x
- Runtime/container: local source checkout + local CLI against live state
- Model/provider: `openai-codex/gpt-5.4` via `openai-codex:rai@rbutera.com`
- Integration/channel (if any): none required; reproduced through `openclaw agent --session-id ...`
- Relevant config (redacted): state rooted at `/Users/rai/.openclaw`, persisted session metadata contained `/home/rai/.openclaw/agents/shikamaru/sessions/<id>.jsonl`

### Steps

1. Seed or retain a `sessions.json` entry for `agent:shikamaru:main` whose `sessionFile` is `/home/rai/.openclaw/agents/shikamaru/sessions/<id>.jsonl`.
2. Run `openclaw agent --agent shikamaru --session-id <id> --message "smoke" --json` on macOS with state rooted at `/Users/rai/.openclaw`.
3. Observe the failure before this patch, then rerun after the patch.

### Expected

- OpenClaw remaps the stale same-agent path into the current state dir and writes to the current session file.

### Actual

- Before the patch, embedded execution attempted `mkdir '/home/rai/.openclaw/agents/shikamaru/sessions'` and failed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - added and ran the new unit regression test
  - ran `pnpm test -- src/config/sessions.test.ts src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/config/sessions/targets.test.ts`
  - reproduced the real local dispatch failure before the fix
  - reran `~/navi/bin/navi-openclaw dispatch --agent shikamaru ...` after the fix and session-metadata repair; it returned `ok: true`
- Edge cases checked:
  - existing cross-agent cross-root fallback tests still pass
  - same-agent remap only applies when the parsed absolute path targets exactly one file under the same agent's sessions directory
- What you did **not** verify:
  - a full end-to-end migration across multiple host OSes from a clean fixture
  - gateway-only success without embedded fallback

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a malformed absolute path that merely resembles a same-agent sessions path could be remapped when it should instead fail fast.
  - Mitigation: the remap path is intentionally narrow; it only applies when the parsed path targets the same agent and exactly one file below `sessions/`.

target.title: Fix Codex message-tool-only source reply completion
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Codex `message_tool_only` turns over iMessage could send the visible reply through the `message` tool, then keep the turn open because OpenClaw did not recognize that tool result as the source reply. That left the run waiting until the no-visible-answer path emitted an incomplete-turn fallback.

This was visible in live iMessage proof as a sent reply followed by `incomplete turn detected` and a suppressed fallback error.

## Why This Change Was Made

The fix teaches the Codex dynamic tool bridge to mark delivered `message_tool_only` source replies as terminal only when the delivery evidence belongs to the current source route/message:

- passes the current message id and source-reply mode into Codex dynamic tool handling
- recognizes current-source `message` tool replies from route matches, native target segments, provider/channel context, and current-message reply receipts
- keeps explicit non-source routes non-terminal, even when a receipt mentions the current message id
- avoids using prior message-send telemetry or bare route/target telemetry as terminal delivery evidence
- preserves tool-owned terminal message results

## User Impact

Users who run Codex through a messaging channel configured with `sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "message_tool_only"` benefit. For iMessage, this is the setup where Codex replies by calling the message tool instead of returning a final text answer for OpenClaw to send afterward.

In that setup, OpenClaw can complete the turn immediately after the message tool returns confirmed source-reply delivery instead of waiting for the incomplete-turn fallback path.

## Performance Savings

The measured live proof removed the post-delivery incomplete/fallback wait from the successful source-reply path. In the failing run, the iMessage `message` tool released at `17:53:40.784`, but OpenClaw did not mark the turn terminal; it logged `incomplete turn detected` at `17:53:41.389` and then suppressed the fallback error at `17:53:41.597`. That left about `0.6s` to incomplete detection and about `0.8s` to fallback after the visible reply had already been sent.

In the fixed run, the iMessage tool output returned at `19:31:07.225`, terminal release happened at `19:31:07.231`, and the message finished processing at `19:31:07.449`. That is about `6ms` from confirmed delivery to terminal recognition and about `224ms` from confirmed delivery to processed completion, while also eliminating the spurious fallback/error path.

## Evidence

Focused local validation on the clean branch after rebasing onto latest `origin/main`:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-message-tool-source-reply.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`
  - `src/agents/embedded-agent-message-tool-source-reply.test.ts`: 19/19 passed
  - `extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tools.test.ts` + `event-projector.test.ts`: 168/168 passed
- `git diff --check`
- autoreview: clean, no accepted/actionable findings reported

Live iMessage proof from the equivalent patched build:

- Failing pre-fix run, message `861`:
  - received `17:53:23.516`
  - run started `17:53:26.657`
  - message tool release `17:53:40.784`
  - `incomplete turn detected` at `17:53:41.389`
  - suppressed error fallback at `17:53:41.597`
  - processed duration `18.115s`
- Fixed run, message `873`:
  - received `19:30:48.452`
  - `message_start` `19:30:48.474`
  - run started `19:30:50.276`
  - Codex emitted `message` tool call at `19:31:01.790`
  - iMessage tool output returned at `19:31:07.225` with `{ ok: true, messageId: "95F25ADC-FA98-4FBC-8988-EB9A8018143F", repliedTo: "A873644F-204D-4807-8C3E-C14B50EE4A4A" }`
  - terminal release at `19:31:07.231`
  - run completed `19:31:07.375`
  - message processed `19:31:07.449`, duration `18.994s`
  - no `incomplete turn detected`
  - no suppressed error fallback

target.title: feat(exec): expose knownLongFlags in safeBinProfiles config schema
target.body:
## Summary

Expose the `knownLongFlags` field in the `safeBinProfiles` config schema, allowing operators to declare long flags as "known" for GNU abbreviation resolution without misusing `allowedValueFlags` or `deniedFlags`.

Closes #56805

> **Rebased 2026-06-10.** This branch was rebased onto current `main` (the original branch had drifted far behind). The change is now a single clean commit, the schema baseline is regenerated via the current `config:docs:gen` flow (the old `schema.base.generated.ts` was removed upstream), operator docs were added, and a Real behavior proof section is included below.

## Problem

The `knownLongFlags` field exists internally in `SafeBinProfile` and is used by `validateSafeBinArgv` for GNU long-option abbreviation resolution. However, it is not present in:

1. The Zod schema (`ToolExecSafeBinProfileSchema`) — `.strict()` rejects it as an unknown key
2. The `SafeBinProfileFixture` type — no way to pass it through config normalization

For empty profiles `{}`, `knownLongFlags` auto-derives as `[]`, causing **all** long flags to be treated as unknown and rejected. The only workaround is to list every possible long flag in `allowedValueFlags` or `deniedFlags`, which changes their semantics.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags: z.array(z.string()).optional()` to `ToolExecSafeBinProfileSchema` |
| `src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy-profiles.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags` to `SafeBinProfileFixture`; normalize it in `normalizeSafeBinProfileFixture`; merge user-provided with auto-derived flags in `compileSafeBinProfile`, filtering strict prefixes of denied flags |
| `src/config/normalize-exec-safe-bin.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags` to the type cast in normalization |
| `docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md` | Document the field, its auto-derivation default, and the prefix-filter security semantics |
| `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256` | Regenerated via `pnpm config:docs:gen` for the new field |
| `src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` | 8 new tests for the config path + security filter |

## Design Decisions

- **Merge, don't replace**: User-provided `knownLongFlags` are merged with auto-derived flags from `allowedValueFlags ∪ deniedFlags`, so flags in those lists stay "known" even if not explicitly listed.
- **Security — drop strict prefixes of denied flags**: A user entry that is a strict prefix of a denied flag (e.g. `--rec` when `--recursive` is denied) is filtered at compile time, so it cannot gain exact-match priority in `resolveCanonicalLongFlag` and bypass the denial. (Addresses the prior Codex review P1.)
- **Reuses `normalizeFixtureFlags`**: Same normalization (trim, dedupe, sort) as other flag arrays.
- **Backwards-compatible**: Existing configs without `knownLongFlags` work identically via auto-derivation.

## Usage

```json5
{
  tools: {
    exec: {
      safeBins: ["mycli"],
      safeBinProfiles: {
        mycli: {
          allowedValueFlags: ["--limit"],
          deniedFlags: ["--recursive"],
          knownLongFlags: ["--dry-run", "--verbose"],
        },
      },
    },
  },
}
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Setting `tools.exec.safeBinProfiles.<bin>.knownLongFlags` in a real config previously could not be accepted — the strict Zod schema had no such key. After this patch the field is accepted by config validation and is honored by the safe-bin argv validator (declared long flags resolve, including GNU abbreviation), while strict prefixes of denied flags are still blocked.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw build from this branch, `OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (bb6e477)`, Node v22.22.0, run via the real `openclaw` CLI and the compiled `exec-safe-bin-policy` module (not mocks).

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
# 1) Real CLI accepts a config containing knownLongFlags
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/klf-proof.json5 openclaw config validate --json
# config: { tools: { exec: { safeBins: ["mycli"], safeBinProfiles: {
#   mycli: { allowedValueFlags: ["--limit"], deniedFlags: ["--recursive"],
#            knownLongFlags: ["--dry-run", "--verbose"] } } } } }

# 2) Same CLI still rejects an unknown key (the pre-fix state of knownLongFlags)
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/klf-proof-unknown.json5 openclaw config validate --json
#   ( mycli: { knownLongFlagsTYPO: ["--dry-run"] } )

# 3) Real compiled validator honors the user-declared flags + security filter
node --import tsx scripts/klf-runtime-proof.mts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
# step 1 — openclaw config validate (knownLongFlags present)
{"valid":true,"path":"/tmp/klf-proof.json5"}

# step 2 — openclaw config validate (unknown key knownLongFlagsTYPO)
{"valid":false,"path":"/tmp/klf-proof-unknown.json5",
 "issues":[{"path":"tools.exec.safeBinProfiles.mycli","message":"Invalid input"}]}

# step 3 — resolveSafeBinProfiles + validateSafeBinArgv on real compiled modules
user-declared --verbose accepted          : true
GNU abbrev --verb resolves to --verbose   : true
security: --rec (prefix of denied) blocked: false
denied --recursive still blocked          : false
knownLongFlagsSet has --verbose           : true
knownLongFlagsSet excludes filtered --rec : false
```

**Observed result after fix:** The live `openclaw config validate` returns `valid: true` for a config carrying `knownLongFlags`, and still returns `valid: false` for an unknown sibling key — confirming the field is genuinely wired into the strict schema. At runtime the compiled validator accepts the user-declared `--verbose`, resolves the `--verb` abbreviation to it, keeps `--recursive` denied, and drops the `--rec` prefix so it cannot bypass that denial.

**What was not tested:** End-to-end exec of a real third-party binary through the approval pipeline (requires a trusted-dir executable); behavior is covered at the policy/validator layer instead.

## Test Plan

- [x] All tests in `exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` pass: **98 passed** (90 existing + 8 new) under `vitest.infra.config.ts`
- [x] `pnpm config:docs:check` and `pnpm config:schema:check` pass (baseline in sync, no drift)
- [x] `oxlint` clean on changed files; no new type errors introduced
- [x] Real behavior proof captured above via the `openclaw` CLI and compiled validator
